Your body makes about 2 million new red blood cells every second, and each one lives for roughly 4 months.
Adults have 206 bones, but babies are born with around 300. Many fuse together as we grow.
More than half of your bones are in your hands and feet. Each hand alone has 27.
Your heart beats roughly 100,000 times a day and about 2.5 billion times over an average lifetime.
Spread flat, the air sacs in your lungs would cover an area about the size of a tennis court.
The smallest bone in your body, the stapes in your ear, is about the size of a grain of rice.
Your femur, the thigh bone, is the longest and strongest bone and can support 30 times your body weight.
Nerve signals can travel along your body at speeds over 400 kilometres per hour.
Every day your kidneys filter about 180 litres of blood, yet you only produce around 2 litres of urine.
The human eye can distinguish an estimated 10 million different colours.
Fingernails grow about four times faster than toenails, and faster on your dominant hand.
Laid end to end, all the blood vessels in an adult body would stretch about 100,000 kilometres.
The acid in your stomach has a pH of around 1.5 to 3.5, similar to battery acid.
Your brain uses about 20 percent of your body's energy despite being only about 2 percent of your weight.
The human brain contains roughly 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of others.
You are slightly taller in the morning. Spinal discs compress during the day and rebound overnight.
Astronauts can grow up to 5 centimetres taller in space as their spines expand without gravity.
The strongest muscle for its size is the masseter, your jaw muscle, which can clench with huge force.
Your small intestine is about 6 metres long, roughly three to four times your own height.
Taste and smell are so linked that food seems flavourless when your nose is blocked.
You blink about 15 to 20 times a minute, which adds up to millions of blinks a year.
Blinking briefly shuts off vision, yet your brain edits out the gaps so you never notice.
The cornea is one of the only parts of your body with no blood supply. It takes oxygen from the air.
Goosebumps are a leftover reflex from ancestors whose raised fur made them look bigger and kept them warm.
Your skin is your largest organ, weighing around 3.5 kilograms and covering about 2 square metres.
The entire outer layer of your skin is replaced roughly every 2 to 4 weeks.
Red blood cells have no nucleus, which frees up space to carry more oxygen.
Your liver can regrow to full size even after up to two thirds of it is removed.
Wisdom teeth are shrinking evolutionary leftovers, and some people are born without them entirely.
The human body contains about 37 trillion cells.
You have more bacterial cells living on and in you than you have human cells.
The average adult has about 5 litres of blood circulating through the body.
Your body produces around 1 to 1.5 litres of saliva every day.
Enamel on your teeth is the hardest substance your body makes, harder than bone.
Unlike bone, tooth enamel cannot repair itself once it is damaged.
The fastest muscles in your body are the ones that move your eyes.
Your eyes make tiny jerky movements called saccades, several times per second, even when you think they are still.
Newborn babies cannot make tears. Crying without tears is normal for the first few weeks.
About 60 percent of the human body is water.
Your brain is roughly 75 percent water, and even mild dehydration can affect concentration.
The heart has its own electrical system and can keep beating briefly even when disconnected from the brain.
Humans are the only animals with chins, and scientists still debate why we have them.
The loudest recorded human snore reached around 111 decibels, louder than a low-flying jet.
Your sense of smell is closely tied to memory because smell signals reach the brain's memory centres directly.
The average person walks the equivalent of about five times around the Earth in a lifetime.
Your peripheral vision is almost colourblind. Most colour is detected only near the centre of your gaze.
The pupil of your eye can change size in about a third of a second in response to light.
A full bladder holds around 400 to 600 millilitres before you feel a strong urge to go.
Your ears never stop working, even in deep sleep, which is why sounds can wake you.
The three tiniest bones in the body are all inside the middle ear.
Earwax is a natural cleaner and antibacterial, slowly carrying dust and debris out of the ear.
Your hair grows about 1.25 centimetres a month, or roughly 15 centimetres a year.
Each hair follicle on your scalp can grow around 20 individual hairs over your lifetime.
You have about 100,000 hairs on your head, and it is normal to shed up to 100 a day.
Fingerprints form before birth and are shaped partly by how the fetus touches the womb.
Identical twins have different fingerprints despite sharing the same DNA.
Dreaming happens mostly during REM sleep, when your brain is nearly as active as when awake.
During REM sleep your body is temporarily paralysed, which stops you acting out your dreams.
You spend roughly a third of your life asleep, about 26 years for an average lifespan.
Most people cannot remember dreams unless they wake up during or just after them.
Your body temperature naturally dips at night, which helps trigger sleep.
Yawning is contagious, and even reading or thinking about it can make you do it.
The knee-jerk reflex bypasses the brain entirely, running through the spinal cord for speed.
It takes about 200 milliseconds for you to react to something you see.
The human body glows very faintly with visible light, far too dim for our eyes to see.
Your left lung is smaller than your right to make room for your heart.
The appendix, once thought useless, may act as a safe reservoir for helpful gut bacteria.
Muscles can only pull, never push, which is why they work in opposing pairs.
Your grip strength comes mostly from muscles in your forearm, not your hand.
The human body has around 640 named muscles.
Cracking your knuckles is the sound of gas bubbles popping in the joint fluid, not bones grinding.
The heart of a woman typically beats slightly faster than a man's.
Your bones are constantly being broken down and rebuilt. Your skeleton is fully renewed about every 10 years.
The hyoid bone in your throat is the only bone not connected to any other bone.
Babies have a stronger sense of taste than adults and are born with taste buds even on the roof of the mouth.
Your taste buds are replaced every one to two weeks.
There are five basic tastes: sweet, sour, salty, bitter, and umami, the savoury taste.
Spicy heat is not a taste. Chili triggers pain and heat receptors, not taste buds.
The human eye can detect a single candle flame from about 3 kilometres away in the dark.
Your brain flips the image from your eyes, which arrives upside down, so you see the world right way up.
Blushing is the only human expression caused by emotion that shows in the blood vessels of the face.
Anaesthesia in surgery only became common after ether was demonstrated publicly in 1846.
Before antiseptics, surgeons rarely washed their hands, and infection killed many patients after surgery.
The first successful blood transfusion between humans took place in the early 1800s.
Vaccination began in 1796 when Edward Jenner used cowpox to protect against smallpox.
Smallpox is the only human disease ever completely eradicated, declared gone in 1980.
Penicillin, the first true antibiotic, was discovered by accident from mould in 1928.
The stethoscope was invented in 1816 because a doctor felt awkward putting his ear on a patient's chest.
X-rays were discovered by accident in 1895, and the discoverer imaged his wife's hand within weeks.
The word muscle comes from a Latin term for little mouse, from how a flexing muscle ripples under the skin.
Your funny bone is not a bone. The tingling comes from hitting the ulnar nerve at your elbow.
A human liver performs over 500 different functions, from filtering toxins to storing energy.
The heart pumps about 7,500 litres of blood through the body every single day.
Your brain cannot feel pain itself, which is why some brain surgery is done while the patient is awake.
The deepest sleep usually comes in the first few hours of the night, before most dreaming begins.
Fingertips are among the most sensitive parts of the body, packed with thousands of touch receptors.
